coredata.dc:title: DOX-conjugated CQD-based nanosponges for tumor intracellular pH-triggered DOX release and imaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Guoping
Pei, Mingliang
Liu, Peng
coredata.dc:description: Novel tumor nanotheranostics were designed by conjugating doxorubicin (DOX) onto the hydrazine-functionalized crosslinked carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/polyethylene glycol bisacrylate (PEGBA) (C-CQDPEGBA-Hy) nanosponges via an acid-labile imine bond for the tumor intracellular real-time imaging and pH-triggered DOX release. The proposed prodrug nanotheranostics with a DOX content of 14.57 % and a mean hydrodynamic diameter around 116 nm showed excellent pH-triggered drug release performance, with a cumulative release of 52.71 % in the simulated tumor intracellular microenvironment in 4 days, while a premature drug leakage of 7.09 % in the simulated normal physiological medium. Besides, the fluorescence could be recovered during the drug release, indicating the potential application for tumor intracellular real-time imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tumor nanotheranostics
Carbon quantum dots
Tumor-specific
DOX delivery
Fluorescent imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: pH-triggered drug release and fluorescence performance
Fabrication and characterization of C-CQDPEGBA-Hy-DOX prodrug nanotheranostics

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Gansu Province

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of CQDs (a), C-CQDPEGBA nanosponges (b) and C-CQDPEGBA-Hy-DOX prodrug nanotheranostics (c).
Hydrodynamic diameters of the CQDs, C-CQDPEGBA, C-CQDPEGBA-Hy, and C-CQDPEGBA-Hy-DOX.
FT-IR spectra of CQDs, C-CQDPEGBA, C-CQDPEGBA-Hy, and C-CQDPEGBA-Hy-DOX (a) and UV–vis spectra of DOX, CQDs, C-CQDPEGBA-Hy, and C-CQDPEGBA-Hy-DOX.

Fluorescence emission spectra of (a) CQDs (0.01 mg/mL), (b) C-CQDPEGBA-Hy (0.01 mg/mL) and (c) C-CQDPEGBA-Hy-DOX (0.01 mg/mL) and (d) C-CQDPEGBA-Hy-DOX (0.2 mg/mL) in pH 5.0 ABS for different times at λex = 360 nm. Inset: digital images of the dispersions under 365 nm UV (left) and daylight (right).
in vitro drug release profiles from the C-CQDPEGBA-Hy-DOX in pH 7.4 PBS and pH 5.0 ABS.
Fabrication of the C-CQDPEGBA-Hy-DOX prodrug nanotheranostics.

head.ce:title: DOX-conjugated CQD-based nanosponges for tumor intracellular pH-triggered DOX release and imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Novel tumor nanotheranostics were designed by conjugating doxorubicin (DOX) onto the hydrazine-functionalized crosslinked carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/polyethylene glycol bisacrylate (PEGBA) (C-CQDPEGBA-Hy) nanosponges via an acid-labile imine bond for the tumor intracellular real-time imaging and pH-triggered DOX release. The proposed prodrug nanotheranostics with a DOX content of 14.57 % and a mean hydrodynamic diameter around 116 nm showed excellent pH-triggered drug release performance, with a cumulative release of 52.71 % in the simulated tumor intracellular microenvironment in 4 days, while a premature drug leakage of 7.09 % in the simulated normal physiological medium. Besides, the fluorescence could be recovered during the drug release, indicating the potential application for tumor intracellular real-time imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: In the last decades, tumor nanotheranostics have attracted more and more interests in tumor chemotherapy and real-time imaging [1]. As a novel member of carbon materials family, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been widely used for the fluorescent bioimaging and theranostics recently [2], owing to its excellent stability, cytocompatibility, and fluorescence property. However, it could not be used for the practical application due to its very small size. To avoid the rapid kidney clearance, the CQD-based tumor nanotheranostics were usually designed as composite nanoparticles with polymers [3,4] and inorganic nanomaterials [5], nanoclusters [6] or nanoassemblies [7].

For the tumor intracellular real-time imaging function, the fluorescence behavior of the CQD-based tumor nanotheranostics is expected to vary after internalization by the tumor cells, either the color or the intensity responding to the microenvironment stimuli. It has been reported that the fluorescence of CQDs could be quenched through a static quenching by transforming the amino groups on the CQDs into Schiff’s base with benzaldehyde derivatives [8] or dynamic quenching by the interaction with the CO and NH groups of CQDs with the adsorbed quinone structure [9]. With the widely used chemotherapeutic drug doxorubicin (DOX) as an example, the conjugated or adsorbed DOX molecules could be released in the acidic tumor intracellular microenvironment, as a result, the fluorescence of CQDs could be recovered for fluorescent imaging.

However, the content of amino groups in CQDs is usually low, which leads to a low drug content in the CQD-based prodrugs with the static quenching approach. As for the dynamic quenching method, DOX is loaded onto the CQD-based drug carriers via the non-covalent bonds, such as hydrogen bond, electrostatic interaction or hydrophobic interaction, which would cause significant premature drug leakage in the normal physiological medium such as blood circulation.

Here, the C-CQDPEGBA nanosponges were designed as fluorescent framework by the interparticle crosslinking of the CQDs with polyethylene glycol bisacrylate (PEGBA) via facile Michael addition reaction. After amidation the carboxyl groups in the CQDs with hydrazine and subsequent conjugation with DOX via an acid-labile imine bond (Scheme 1 ), the proposed C-CQDPEGBA-Hy-DOX prodrug nanotheranostics were obtained for the tumor intracellular real-time imaging and pH-triggered DOX release. Compared with the reported pH-responsive CQD-based composites for the tumor intracellular imaging and drug release [10–15], the proposed strategy was more simple and convenient. Most importantly, the CQDs fluorescence could be recovered after the drug release in the simulated tumor intracellular medium, making the proposed prodrug a promising nanotheranostics for the tumor intracellular real-time imaging owing to the enhanced fluorescent intensity in the tumor intracellular medium than the normal physiological medium.

Hydroxyl-polyethylene glycol-hydroxyl (HO-PEG-OH, Mn = 2000, 99 %) was purchased from Beijing Kaizheng Biotechnology Developing Co., Ltd. Acryloyl chloride (95 %) was obtained from Aladdin Chem. Co. Hydrazine hydrate (N2H4·H2O, >80.0 %) was bought from Tianjin Guangfu Fine Chemical Research Institute. Doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX·HCl, 99.4 %) was obtained from Beijing Huafeng United Technology Co., Ltd. Other reagents were all analytical grade and used directly without any treatment. Double distilled water was used throughout.

The N-doped CQDs were prepared via the microwave method with sodium citrate and ethylenediamine as raw materials [6]. PEGBA was synthesized by the esterification of HO-PEG-OH with acryloyl chloride. 1H NMR (Fig. S1, 400 MHz, CDCl3, δ, ppm): δ = 6.32 (2H, 2CH2=CH), δ = 6.17 (2H, 2CH 2=CH), δ = 5.62 (2H, 2CH 2=CH), δ = 3.65 (4H, CH 2CH2 O).

The C-CQDPEGBA nanosponges were prepared by stirring 10 mL of DMSO solution containing 40 mg of PEGBA and 36 mg of CQDs at 30 °C in dark for 72 h. The earth-yellow product was collected by dialysis the mixture against water (MWCO: 1000) for 3 days, and lyophilization.

Then the C-CQDPEGBA nanosponges were functionalized with hydrazine by stirring 15 mL of the aqueous solution containing 60 mg of C-CQDPEGBA, 40 mg of EDC•HCl, 16 mg of NHS, and 1.0 mL of hydrazine hydrate at 30 °C in dark for 48 h. The yellow C-CQDPEGBA-Hy nanosponges were collected by dialysis against water (MWCO: 1000) for 3 days, and lyophilization.

Finally, the red C-CQDPEGBA-Hy-DOX prodrug nanotheranostics were fabricated by stirring 15 mL of DMSO solution containing 40 mg of C-CQDPEGBA-Hy, 40 mg of DOX, 200 μL of triethylamine and 20 μL of trifluoroacetic acid in N2 at room temperature in dark for 24 h, followed with dialysis (MWCO: 14000) for 5 days and lyophilization.
1H NMR analysis was carried out on a JEOL ECS 400 MHz NMR spectrometer.
Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR) was performed on a Bruker IFS 66 v/s infrared spectrometer.
UV–vis adsorption and DOX concentration were detected on a TU-1901 UV–vis spectrometer.
Fluorescent emission spectra were recorded on a Hitachi F-7500 fluorescence spectrometer with aqueous dispersions.
The hydrodynamic diameter measurements were performed on a BI-200SM equipment.
The morphology of the sample was observed on a JEM1200 EX/S transmission electron microscope (TEM).

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH-triggered drug release and fluorescence performance
ce:section.ce:para: The pH-triggered drug release was in vitro evaluated by dislyzing 10 mL of the 0.2 mg/mL C-CQDPEGBA-Hy-DOX solution with a dialysis bag (MWCO: 1000) at 37 °C in the buffer solution with 100 mL of the corresponding buffer solution: pH 7.4 PBS mimicking the normal physiological medium and pH 5.0 ABS mimicking the tumor intracellular microenvironment respectively.

During the drug release, the dialysate was measured with UV–vis spectrometer at 480 nm to calculate the cumulative drug release. Besides, the fluorescence of the dispersion in the dialysis bag in pH 5.0 ABS was recorded on fluorescence spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and characterization of C-CQDPEGBA-Hy-DOX prodrug nanotheranostics
ce:section.ce:para: Then the carboxyl groups of CQDs in the C-CQDPEGBA nanosponges were amidated with hydrazine hydrate. Clearly, the stretching vibration of CO (carboxylic acid groups) at 1705 cm−1 became very weak after the reaction (Fig. 3 a) [17], demonstrating the functionalization with hydrazine. Furthermore, the Dh of the product was 125.1 nm, near to the C-CQDPEGBA nanosponges. It indicated that the fluorescent framework had been reserved in the functionalization, in which the ester bonds in the PEG linkers did not react with hydrazine under the mild condition.

Finally, DOX was conjugated onto the C-CQDPEGBA-Hy nanosponges via the imine conjugation between the hydrazine groups in the nanosponges and carbonyl group in DOX. A new absorption band at 1578 cm−1 appeared in the FT-IR spectrum of the product, ascribable to the imine stretching vibration [18]. The obtained C-CQDPEGBA-Hy-DOX prodrug nanotheranostics showed a spherical morphology with mean particle size of 72.4 ± 3.6 nm (Fig. 1c). The size was bigger than the C-CQDPEGBA nanosponges, because of the conjugation of DOX. However, the Dh of the C-CQDPEGBA-Hy-DOX prodrug nanotheranostics was smaller than the nanosponges, due to the nanosponges became more hydrophobic after DOX conjugation. The DOX content was determined as 14.57 % on UV/vis spectrometer after DOX had been completely released in an extreme condition (1.0 M HCl aqueous solution) for 24 h [19].

The UV–vis spectra of the samples are presented in Fig. 3b. The CQDs showed two absorption peaks at 240 nm and 355 nm, ascribed to the π˗π* and n˗π* transition, respectively [6]. The two peaks could be seen in the C-CQDPEGBA-Hy nanosponges, however, the adsorption peak at 355 nm became very weak with emerged one of DOX at 480 nm after conjugating DOX.

The CQDs showed an excitation-independent fluorescence property, with maximum PL intensity at excitation wavelength of 360 nm (Fig. 4 a). The PL intensity decreased in the C-CQDPEGBA-Hy nanosponges, due to the reaction on the surface carboxyl groups in CQDs (Fig. 4b). After DOX conjugation, the PL intensity decreased distinctly in the final C-CQDPEGBA-Hy-DOX prodrug nanotheranostics (Fig. 4c), maybe due to the fluorescence quenching with DOX. Such characteristics are desired for the fluorescent imaging after tumor intracellular pH-triggered DOX release.

The pH-triggered drug release was assessed in vitro at 37 °C in the simulated normal physiological medium (pH 7.4 PBS) and simulated tumor intracellular microenvironment (pH 5.0 ABS). Owing to the consumption of the surface carboxyl groups in CQDs by the functionalization with hydrazine, the drug loading via electrostatic interaction could be efficiently avoided. No obvious burst release could be seen in the two release profiles (Fig. 5 ). A premature drug leakage of 7.09 % was found in the simulated normal physiological medium in 4 days. The drug leakage in the present work was much lower than the reported pH-responsive CQD-based composites for the tumor intracellular imaging and drug release, in the form of prodrugs via acid-labile conjugations [10,11] or nanocarriers for the non-covalent drug loading [12–15]. Such low drug leakage is expected to minimize the toxic side effects of DOX on the normal cells and tissues.

As in the weak acidic medium, the drug release was fast in the first 54 h with a cumulative release of 48.66 %, due to the acid-triggered cleavage of the imine conjugation [18]. After that, a slow sustained drug release could be seen, and a cumulative release of 52.71 % was achieved within 4 days. The slow sustained drug release should be caused by the two reasons: i) the slow diffusion of the released DOX out of the nanosponges, and ii) the cleavage/formation equilibrium of the dynamic covalent conjugation, as well as the adsorption of the released DOX onto the nanosponges.

The drug release profiles were fitted with the Higuchi and Korsmeyer-Peppas models (Fig. S2). In both the releasing media, the higher correlation coefficients were obtained with the Higuchi equation than the Korsmeyer-Peppas equation, demonstrating a better fitting with the Higuchi equation of the diffusion mechanism [20]. The result could also explain the reason i) for the slow sustained drug release behavior.

During drug release in pH 5.0 ABS, the fluorescence behavior of the dispersion of the C-CQDPEGBA-Hy-DOX prodrug nanotheranostics in the dialysis bag was also recorded, as shown in Fig. 4d. The PL intensity increased distinctly with prolonging the releasing time, demonstrating the transformation of the prodrug nanotheranostics into the C-CQDPEGBA nanosponges fluorescent framework due to the cleavage of the imine conjugation and DOX release in the acidic medium. The feature of fluorescence recovery is expected to be used for the tumor intracellular real-time imaging-guided drug release.

With a much higher concentration (0.2 mg/mL), the PL intensity of the prodrug nanotheranostics after drug release for 48 h was lower than the C-CQDPEGBA nanosponges with a lower concentration (0.01 mg/mL), due to the dynamic quenching of the CQDs by adsorbing the released DOX molecules [9]. It further indicated the reason ii) for the slow sustained drug release behavior.

In summary, novel CQD-based prodrug nanotheranostics were fabricated by conjugating DOX onto the C-CQDPEGBA-Hy nanosponges via an acid-labile imine. In the in vitro drug release, an excellent pH-triggered drug release was achieved with very low premature drug leakage and their fluorescence could be recovered during the drug release. Such features make the proposed prodrug nanotheranostics promising potential for the tumor intracellular real-time imaging and pH-triggered DOX release.
Guoping Li: Investigation, Data curation. Mingliang Pei: Investigation. Peng Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Visualization, Writing - review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Gansu Province, China (Grant No. 18JR3RA271).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfa.2020.125258.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
